content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 105v–105r | **Source Images:** DEU_010_page_001.jpg, DEU_010_page_002.jpg, DEU_010_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** At that time the LORD said to me, "Carve two stone tablets like the first ones and come up to me on the mountain. Also make a wooden ark.

**2** I will write on the tablets the words that were on the first tablets, which you shattered. Then you are to place them in the ark."

**3** So I made an ark of acacia wood and carved two stone tablets like the first ones, and I went up the mountain with the two tablets in my hands.

**4** The LORD wrote on the tablets the same words as before — the Ten Commandments that the LORD had proclaimed to you on the mountain from the midst of the fire on the day of the assembly — and the LORD gave them to me.

**5** Then I turned and came down from the mountain and placed the tablets in the ark I had made, and there they remain, just as the LORD commanded me.

**6** (The Israelites journeyed from Beeroth-bene-jaakan to Moserah. Aaron died there and was buried there, and his son Eleazar succeeded him as priest.

**7** From there they journeyed to Gudgodah, and from Gudgodah to Jotbathah, a land with streams of water.

**8** At that time the LORD set apart the tribe of Levi to carry the ark of the covenant of the LORD, to stand before the LORD to minister to him, and to pronounce blessings in his name — as they still do today.

**9** That is why the Levites have no share or inheritance among their brothers — the LORD himself is their inheritance, as the LORD your God told them.)

**10** As for me — I stayed on the mountain as I had before, forty days and forty nights, and the LORD listened to me again. He was not willing to destroy you.

**11** Then the LORD said to me, "Get up, lead the people on their journey so that they may go in and take possession of the land I swore to give their ancestors."

**12** And now, Israel, what is the LORD your God asking of you? Simply this: to hold the LORD your God in reverent awe, to walk in all his ways, to love him, to serve the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your life,

**13** and to keep the commands and statutes of the LORD that I am giving you today for your own good.

**14** Look — the heavens, even the highest heavens, belong to the LORD your God, as does the earth and everything in it.

**15** Yet the LORD chose your ancestors and loved them above all other peoples, and he chose you, their descendants, out of all nations — and so it is today.

**16** Circumcise your hearts, then, and stop being so stubborn.

**17** For the LORD your God is the God of gods and the Lord of lords — the great, mighty, and awesome God who shows no partiality and takes no bribes.

**18** He defends the cause of the fatherless and the widow, and he loves the sojourner among you, giving them food and clothing.

**19** So you are to love the sojourner — because you yourselves were sojourners in Egypt.

**20** Hold the LORD your God in reverent awe. Serve him. Cling to him. Take your oaths in his name.

**21** He is your glory and he is your God, who did for you these great and awesome things your own eyes saw.

**22** Your ancestors went down to Egypt as seventy persons, and now the LORD your God has made you as numerous as the stars in the sky.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1–5 — The second tablets as restoration narrative:** What Moses telescopes in four verses is a remarkable act of divine grace: after the shattering of the first tablets (ch. 9), God offers restoration rather than permanent rupture. The ark itself is introduced here matter-of-factly — Moses makes it himself, of acacia wood — signaling that the covenant's preservation is a shared human-divine project. The LXX follows the MT closely.

**V. 4 — "The Ten Commandments" for aseret ha-devarim:** Literally "the ten words" — the Decalogue. PNEUMA uses "Ten Commandments" as the most recognizable rendering in English, though "ten words" is more precise. The LXX uses dekalogue (τα δεκα λογια) here in substance.

**V. 6–9 — The Levitical parenthesis:** These verses are a third-person historical note embedded in Moses' first-person address — a narrative inset about Aaron's death and the Levitical office. PNEUMA sets this apart with parentheses, as many modern translations do, to alert readers to the shift in voice without interrupting the flow of Moses' speech.

**V. 12 — "What is the LORD your God asking of you?":** The Hebrew mah YHWH Elohecha sho'el me'imecha — this is Moses' great rhetorical compression: after lengthy warnings and history, he pivots to "what does it all come down to?" The answer is a five-part list: awe, walking, love, service, obedience. PNEUMA renders "holding in reverent awe" for yir'ah (fear/awe) to capture the worshipful rather than merely terror-filled sense. The LXX uses phobou (to fear/revere), which covers the same range.

**V. 16 — "Circumcise your hearts" (mul et orlat levavkhem):** This is among the most theologically charged metaphors in Deuteronomy. Circumcision was the physical sign of covenant entry; Moses now internalizes it: the real covenant mark must be cut into the heart — removing the covering of hardness and resistance. PNEUMA preserves the imperative directly: "Circumcise your hearts, then." The LXX reads peritmethesethe (you shall be circumcised), using the same verb but passive, which interestingly shifts agency — Jeremiah 4:4 and Deuteronomy 30:6 will pick up both the command and promise forms.

**V. 17 — "God of gods and Lord of lords":** Elohei ha-elohim va-Adonei ha-adonim. This is one of the Bible's great doxological assertions — the absolute supremacy of Israel's God over any rival divine claim. PNEUMA preserves the double superlative structure. The LXX reads theos ton theon kai kyrios ton kyrion.

**V. 17–19 — Power and vulnerability:** PNEUMA notes the remarkable juxtaposition: the cosmic sovereign (v. 17) is the very same God who champions the fatherless, widow, and sojourner (v. 18–19). This is no remote deity. Divine greatness expresses itself in care for the most vulnerable. The sojourner (ger) appears as a touchstone of covenant ethics — love for the vulnerable is grounded in memory: "you were sojourners in Egypt."

**V. 20 — "Cling to him" (u-vo tidbaq):** The Hebrew d-b-q is the same root used in Genesis 2:24 for the husband "clinging" to his wife in covenant union. PNEUMA renders "cling" to preserve the relational intimacy — not just formal loyalty but ardent attachment.

— Signed, PNEUMA
